video_title: Anthropic Files $965B IPO, Trump Signs AI Executive Order, and ChatGPT Crosses 1B Users | EP #262
gist: WTF Just Happened in Tech panel (Diamandis, Dave Blundin, Alex Wissner-Gross, Imad Mostaque) reacts to Anthropic's confidential trillion-dollar-plus IPO filing, Trump's deregulatory AI executive order, ChatGPT hitting 1B MAU, Microsoft's in-house model push, Nvidia's move into PC CPUs, Bernie Sanders' AI sovereign-wealth-fund bill, and a wave of multi-billion-dollar longevity/gene-therapy news. Core investor thesis: a self-funding 'AI economy' with winner-take-all distribution moats, record capital flows, and trillion-dollar valuations reached in ~5 years.

insights.claim: Anthropic has confidentially filed IPO paperwork with the SEC and could be the first major frontier AI lab to go public, beating OpenAI to the public markets.
insights.speaker: Peter Diamandis (host)
insights.so_what: First pure-play frontier-lab listing creates a tradable public proxy for AI-model economics and a valuation benchmark for OpenAI/xAI; watch for the S-1 and lockups.
insights.quote: Anthropic just confidentially filed IPO paperwork with SEC and could be the first major frontier lab to go public.

insights.claim: Polymarket prices a 60% chance Anthropic surpasses $1.8 trillion market cap on its first trading day, the same figure cited for SpaceX's anticipated IPO.
insights.speaker: Peter Diamandis (host)
insights.so_what: A $1.8T day-one cap implies extreme demand and would set the reference multiple for every other AI listing; prediction-market odds are a real-time sentiment gauge to track into the IPO.
insights.quote: Poly Market gives it a 60% chance that Anthropic surpasses 1.8 trillion in market cap on its first day.

insights.claim: Anthropic is run by only ~5,000 employees, implying ~$9.4M revenue per employee versus ~$2.5M at Apple and ~$2.1M at Google, roughly 4x the best legacy tech firms.
insights.speaker: Peter Diamandis / Dave Blundin
insights.so_what: Extreme revenue-per-employee reframes AI labs as ultra-high-margin compounders and supports the 'exponential organization' thesis; a screen metric for spotting the next outliers.
insights.quote: Enthropic is only 5,000 people. You're talking about an insane amount of money divided 5,000 ways.

insights.claim: Imad Mostaque estimates Anthropic revenue around $50-60B implying roughly a 20x revenue multiple, which he calls high but not extreme, and says $100-150B revenue next year would not surprise anyone.
insights.speaker: Imad Mostaque (guest, founder/CEO Intelligent Internet)
insights.so_what: A ~20x sales multiple on a fast-doubling top line frames the bull case that these valuations are growth-justified, not bubble; the revenue-doubling assumption is the key thing to verify.
insights.quote: Anthropic right now's revenue is probably 60 billion 50 billion 60 billion so it's coming at 20 times revenue which is high but it isn't a palenteer.

insights.claim: These newly-public AI labs will hold unprecedented dry powder and could each do thousands of billion-dollar acquisitions, spawning a whole ecosystem of supplier and target companies.
insights.speaker: Dave Blundin (guest, investor)
insights.so_what: Post-IPO acquisition firepower is a tailwind for adjacent AI-tooling, infra and data startups as acquisition targets; position in the supplier/target ecosystem, not just the labs.
insights.quote: This group of companies can do thousands of billion dollar acquisitions.

insights.claim: Time-to-trillion-dollar valuation has collapsed: Apple took 42 years, Google 21, SpaceX 24, OpenAI ~10, and Anthropic roughly 5 years.
insights.speaker: Peter Diamandis (host)
insights.so_what: Radically compressed value-creation timelines mean equity windows open and close fast; investors must access pre-IPO/early secondary or AI ETFs sooner.

insights.claim: Panel expects the AI/IPO wave to be oversubscribed with roughly a trillion dollars of capital seeking exposure, so SpaceX and Anthropic listings will not face demand shortfalls.
insights.speaker: Dave Blundin (guest)
insights.so_what: If demand is as deep as claimed, allocation scarcity (not price) is the constraint; aftermarket pops are likely and crowding into AI names continues.
insights.quote: No, this will be over subscribed. SpaceX will be oversubscribed.

insights.claim: Blundin frames the AI capital wave as the biggest flow of money in world history by an order of magnitude or more and urges investors not to become numb to trillion-dollar IPOs.
insights.speaker: Dave Blundin (guest)
insights.so_what: Treats AI capital formation as a once-in-history reallocation; argues against complacency and for active participation across the stack.
insights.quote: this flow of money is the biggest in the history of the world by an order of magnitude or more

themes.theme: AI policy: Trump EO, sovereign wealth fund, bio-screening

insights.claim: Trump signed an AI executive order rejecting heavy regulation and asking labs to voluntarily give government access to new models 30 days before public release, with Sam Altman and Anthropic publicly endorsing it.
insights.speaker: Peter Diamandis (host)
insights.so_what: A deregulatory, voluntary-disclosure regime removes a near-term overhang on US lab deployment speed and favors US labs over EU competitors; bullish for US AI capex and release cadence.
insights.quote: President Trump just signed an executive order that basically says America is not going to slow down on AI.

insights.claim: The panel reads the EO as regulatory capture in motion: Altman and Dario lobbied the White House and got the proposed regulation watered down to voluntary self-regulation, analogous to Intel CEO Lip-Bu Tan reversing his position after a White House meeting.
insights.speaker: Dave Blundin (guest)
insights.so_what: Incumbent labs shaping policy entrenches their position; political access becomes a competitive moat investors should weight when assessing lab durability.
insights.quote: They met with the White House and then you have a complete watering down of the regulation

insights.claim: Bernie Sanders is introducing the American AI Sovereign Wealth Fund Act proposing a one-time 50% tax on the stock (not profits) of OpenAI, Anthropic and other major AI firms to give the public an ownership stake.
insights.speaker: Peter Diamandis (host)
insights.so_what: Even if it fails, it shifts the Overton window toward equity/stake mandates as a condition of operating; a tail policy risk for AI-lab cap tables and domicile choices.
insights.quote: require OpenAI, Anthropic, and other major AI companies to contribute 50% of their stock to a public wealth fund

insights.claim: Blundin argues a stock-based sovereign wealth fund is structurally unworkable because the government would have to dump trillions in illiquid AI stock to fund any public benefit, likely within one election cycle, crashing the market.
insights.speaker: Dave Blundin (guest)
insights.so_what: Implies forced-selling/overhang risk if such a fund were ever created; income-tax structures are cleaner, so the equity-stake version is unlikely to survive in current form.
insights.quote: you have to sell the stock to turn that into any public good. You can't dump $5 trillion of stock on the market.

insights.claim: Wissner-Gross argues a US sovereign wealth fund is likely to happen and would naturally hold large stakes in OpenAI, Anthropic and SpaceX via a broad market index, pointing to the administration's existing 10% stake in Intel and golden-share equivalents as precedent.
insights.speaker: Alex Wissner-Gross (guest)
insights.so_what: Signals growing state equity participation in strategic tech; investors should track government stake-taking (Intel precedent) as a structural feature of the AI cycle.
insights.quote: this administration taking 10% stakes in Intel and Golden Share equivalents in other companies as the basis potentially for a sovereign wealth fund

insights.claim: Diamandis co-signed an open letter to Congress with Altman, Amodei, Hassabis and Suleyman calling for laws requiring DNA-synthesis companies to screen orders, with a bipartisan Senate bill already in play.
insights.speaker: Peter Diamandis (host)
insights.so_what: Mandatory DNA-synthesis screening would advantage compliant incumbents like Twist Bioscience that already screen; a rare 'please regulate us' moment creating a regulatory moat in synthetic biology.
insights.quote: this is one of those rare moments where the industry is saying, "Please regulate us."

insights.claim: Mostaque predicts every government will introduce an AI token license and that the most sensible mechanism is taxing models on 'GDPval' (measured GDP value created), rather than equity stakes.
insights.speaker: Imad Mostaque (guest)
insights.so_what: A GDP-value-based token tax would be a recurring cost line for model providers; an alternative policy vector to watch alongside equity-stake proposals.
insights.quote: the most sensible thing is to tax them on GDP eval.

themes.theme: Adoption, distribution moats & second-order market structure

insights.claim: ChatGPT passed 1 billion monthly active users roughly three years after its Nov 2022 launch, faster than YouTube (a decade), Instagram (8 yrs) and TikTok (5 yrs), with OpenAI year-over-year growth at 62%.
insights.speaker: Peter Diamandis (host)
insights.so_what: Fastest consumer-product scaling in history, achieved largely organically without paid acquisition; underpins OpenAI's distribution-moat valuation case.
insights.quote: nothing in history has scaled this fast. Year-over-year growth is at 62% and holding for OpenAI.

insights.claim: Anthropic's Claude is at ~56 million monthly active users but growing ~640% year-over-year, roughly 10x OpenAI's growth rate.
insights.speaker: Peter Diamandis (host)
insights.so_what: Claude's hyper-growth from a smaller base supports the IPO bull case and the 'Claude is Apple, OpenAI is Microsoft' framing; track whether the growth rate holds post-listing.
insights.quote: Claude anthropics model is now at 56 million monthly active users and is growing at 10 times the rate 640% yearon-year.

insights.claim: The panel argues distribution, not model quality, is the dominant moat between frontier labs, citing Altman's reported preference for a billion users over the strongest model, with OpenAI's GPT-5.5 now claimed to lead on both.
insights.speaker: Alex Wissner-Gross (guest)
insights.so_what: If distribution is the durable moat, capital should favor labs with the largest user funnels and 'super-app' agents; raw model leadership is a transient edge.
insights.quote: distribution ultimately was a stronger mode, a stronger advantage and differentiation than having the strongest model weights.

insights.claim: The panel predicts the most valuable AI product will be the personal coordinating 'Jarvis' agent next to the user that orchestrates all other agents, making it the focal point of customer-acquisition spend.
insights.speaker: Imad Mostaque / panel
insights.so_what: The orchestrating consumer agent ('Who is your Jarvis?') is framed as the only game in town; whoever owns it captures distribution rents, a key strategic battleground to watch.
insights.quote: the most important agent is the agent that's next to you that's coordinating all the other agents.

insights.claim: Mostaque argues the AI economy is now funded to live within itself: post-IPO labs can spend $100B directly into an agent economy (e.g., on diseases) generating internal cash flow without touching JPMorgan, Main Street or legacy business.
insights.speaker: Imad Mostaque (guest)
insights.so_what: A self-contained, self-funding AI economy could decouple AI value creation from the legacy economy and from traditional capital intermediaries; reframes circularity concerns as a feature.
insights.quote: this conduit of capital through these IPOs back into the pure AI world, it could entirely build an economy of its own

insights.claim: Mostaque says a new lab can still catch OpenAI/Anthropic on algorithms but it is very hard because the incumbents' data and distribution advantages, which they will spend hundreds of billions to lock down, make it a winner-take-all go-to-market race.
insights.speaker: Imad Mostaque (guest)
insights.so_what: Frames frontier AI as a winner-take-most market gated by distribution/capital, not just model quality; argues against betting on unknown challenger labs absent a novel distribution channel.
insights.quote: distribution effects count for so much... this is typically how we see markets in terms of winner takes all

insights.claim: Apollo Global Management chief economist Torsten Slok says AI is a net job creator and that the actual employment data show jobs are not yet being displaced, contradicting job-apocalypse headlines.
insights.speaker: Peter Diamandis (host, citing Torsten Slok)
insights.so_what: If labor-market disruption is slower than feared (Cognizant hiring 20,000 grads), the macro/consumer backdrop stays supportive of AI capex narratives near-term; watch the data/narrative divergence.
insights.quote: AI is a net job creator. companies citing AI to just to justify cuts

insights.claim: Blundin reports employment across his portfolio companies is up 2x, not down, because AI lets non-engineers (e.g., insurance experts at healthcare.com) build products without engineers, expanding the builder pool.
insights.speaker: Dave Blundin (guest)
insights.so_what: Ground-level signal that AI is currently additive to headcount in fast-growing startups; supports the 'become a builder/entrepreneur' thesis over near-term automation fears.
insights.quote: employment across our network of companies is up 2x, not down.

insights.claim: Microsoft dropped seven in-house AI models at Build 2026 built from scratch (no OpenAI distillation), including an Excel-tuned model said to match GPT-5.4 at 10x efficiency, plus a Mayo Clinic healthcare-model collaboration.
insights.speaker: Peter Diamandis (host)
insights.so_what: Signals Microsoft moving from OpenAI distribution layer to full-stack owner, reducing OpenAI dependence; a structural shift in the Microsoft-OpenAI relationship worth pricing into both.
insights.quote: Microsoft is saying we're not just a distribution layer for OpenAi anymore... we're building the whole stack.

insights.claim: The panel judges Microsoft's new models as mid-tier (roughly at a good Chinese-lab level, competitive with Anthropic/OpenAI models from months ago) and concludes Microsoft is compute- and talent-starved and 'not in the game' at the frontier.
insights.speaker: Alex Wissner-Gross / Imad Mostaque (guests)
insights.so_what: Bearish on Microsoft's frontier-AI standing despite the headline launch; frames Microsoft as office/productivity-specialized rather than AGI-bound, a key differentiator for relative positioning.
insights.quote: Where Microsoft is right now is they're at the level of a Chinese lab. a good Chinese lab and I don't think they're going to go that much further

insights.claim: Wissner-Gross frames frontier AI as a battle of people not brands, noting Zuckerberg's reported billion-dollar comp offer to OpenAI's Mark Chen was turned down and Microsoft cannot match such packages to recruit frontier talent.
insights.speaker: Dave Blundin / Alex Wissner-Gross (guests)
insights.so_what: Talent compensation wars are the real moat fight; lab valuations should be assessed on ability to retain key researchers, not corporate brand or balance sheet.
insights.quote: Can Microsoft offer Mark Chen a billion dollar comp package? Like, are you kidding me?

insights.claim: Nvidia is launching its first ARM-based PC processors, the N1 and N1X, with the N1X offering 20 CPU cores and 6,144 CUDA cores putting GPU performance on par with a laptop RTX 5070, a direct shot at Apple, Intel and AMD.
insights.speaker: Peter Diamandis (host)
insights.so_what: Nvidia attacking the laptop CPU market threatens Intel/AMD/Apple silicon share and gives Nvidia first direct-to-consumer data; a competitive-positioning catalyst across PC semis.
insights.quote: This is the direct shot across the bow to Apple, Intel, and AMD.

insights.claim: Wissner-Gross notes Nvidia is now the majority of TSMC bleeding-edge-node transistor output (no longer Apple), suggesting Nvidia's TSMC pipe could make it the gatekeeper of frontier-node compute for laptop vendors.
insights.speaker: Alex Wissner-Gross (guest)
insights.so_what: If Nvidia controls frontier-node allocation, it gains leverage over the entire device supply chain amid memory/semiconductor shortages; a supply-chain choke-point thesis for Nvidia dominance.
insights.quote: Nvidia is now the majority of transistors coming out of TSMC at their bleeding edge node. It's no longer Apple.

insights.claim: Mostaque frames Nvidia's edge-AI push (N1X, open-sourcing Cosmos and Nemotron) as a defensive move to own the at-home 'Jarvis' substrate and block AMD's Strix Halo integrated 128GB chips.
insights.speaker: Imad Mostaque (guest)
insights.so_what: Edge inference (sufficient to run ~1-3B active-parameter models locally) is an emerging battleground; Nvidia open-sourcing models to drive chip pull-through is a strategy to watch versus AMD.
insights.quote: the play here is Jarvis. It's intelligence throughout the home... to block AMD

insights.claim: Mostaque says physical embodied robotics is a far bigger market than the GPU market and robots will not depreciate on the fast schedule GPUs do, making robotics a structurally better long-duration asset.
insights.speaker: Imad Mostaque (guest)
insights.so_what: If robot capex has longer useful life and a larger TAM than GPUs, it reshapes the AI infrastructure investment case toward embodied hardware; a depreciation-schedule argument for robotics exposure.
insights.quote: It is far bigger than the GPU market like actual physical embodied robots. And they will not have the same depreciation schedule either.

insights.claim: OpenAI is building an in-house robotics team led by ex-Sora lead Aditya Ramesh, redeploying its video researchers to embodied robotics after winding down Sora.
insights.speaker: Peter Diamandis / Imad Mostaque
insights.so_what: OpenAI entering humanoid robotics (mirroring Elon/Tesla) signals frontier labs verticalizing into the physical 'innermost loop' of building data centers and a new robotics data source; track as a multi-year theme.
insights.quote: We are focused on robots to support skilled workers to build our future infrastructure

insights.claim: Wissner-Gross notes Stargate, OpenAI's data-center initiative, has shifted toward leasing rather than building, suggesting Sam Altman may spin up an independent data-center infra effort or revive Stargate via a robotics-construction angle.
insights.speaker: Alex Wissner-Gross (guest)
insights.so_what: A lease-vs-build pivot at Stargate changes who captures data-center capex value (REITs/operators vs OpenAI); relevant for infra and real-estate exposure to the AI build-out.
insights.quote: Stargate Sam's signature initiative to go build the data centers within OpenAI has shifted in the direction of leasing rather than building.

insights.claim: Microsoft AI's Mustafa Suleyman said AI training compute has increased one-trillion-fold with another 1000x coming in the next three years.
insights.speaker: Peter Diamandis (host, citing Mustafa Suleyman)
insights.so_what: A projected further 1000x training-compute scaling underpins sustained demand for GPUs, power and data centers; a directional input for the compute-supply-chain investment thesis.
insights.quote: AI training compute has increased one trillionfold with another thousandx coming in the next 3 years.

insights.claim: Satya Nadella stated Microsoft's closed-loop data-center cooling is filled once and operates with effectively zero ongoing water consumption, using roughly a single restaurant's daily water over a year.
insights.speaker: Peter Diamandis (host, citing Satya Nadella)
insights.so_what: Closed-loop cooling reframes the data-center 'water crisis' narrative; a social-license factor that affects siting approvals and the pace of the AI build-out.
insights.quote: the cooling loop is filled um once and the data center can operate effectively with zero water consumption.

insights.claim: Diamandis cites that California almond farming uses ~1.3 trillion gallons of water per year versus ~150 billion gallons for all US data centers combined, arguing data-center water use is a minor, non-polluting footprint.
insights.speaker: Peter Diamandis (host)
insights.so_what: Quantifies that data-center water concerns are small relative to agriculture; supports the view that 'not in my backyard' protests are a manageable, narrative-driven risk to AI infra siting.
insights.quote: almond farming uses 1.3 trillion gallons per year compared to all the US data centers that use 150 billion gallons per year

insights.claim: Blundin predicts hyperscalers will increasingly offer local communities lower-cost or free electricity as a deal-sweetener to win data-center siting approvals.
insights.speaker: Dave Blundin (guest)
insights.so_what: Community energy give-backs become a cost and negotiation lever in data-center deals; relevant to utility/IPP economics and the local politics of the build-out.
insights.quote: give us free electricity for our 100,000 you know citizens here

insights.claim: Wissner-Gross argues the generality of AGI is starting to shrink as bio and cyber capabilities (e.g., OpenAI's Rosalind biodefense and GPT-cyber/Mythos series) get carved out of general models into restricted, post-trained models available only to governments and trusted researchers.
insights.speaker: Alex Wissner-Gross (guest)
insights.so_what: A bifurcation into gated, capability-restricted models for sovereign customers creates a new high-value enterprise/government revenue tier and regulatory moat; track lab government-contract pipelines.
insights.quote: the G in AGI is starting to shrink. We're seeing bio capabilities that would otherwise be built into general models... get carved out as separate fine-tuned/postrained models

insights.claim: Mostaque notes private-sector AI now performs national-security R&D (zero-day discovery) historically done by the NSA, with OpenAI's cyber-vulnerability and Rosalind models effectively privatizing capabilities with major national-security implications.
insights.speaker: Alex Wissner-Gross / Imad Mostaque
insights.so_what: De facto privatization of sovereign capabilities opens large government/defense AI markets and raises the policy stakes; a structural revenue and regulatory driver for frontier labs.
insights.quote: governmental functions such as discovering zero days at scale which the NSA historically performed are now deacto privatized

insights.claim: Mostaque highlights that public-sector AI token usage is roughly 0.01% of world tokens despite the public sector being ~20% of world GDP, arguing governments must massively ramp public-sector compute.
insights.speaker: Imad Mostaque (guest)
insights.so_what: A large untapped public-sector compute market (e.g., government subsidizing tokens to screen every DNA sequence) is a potential demand catalyst for inference providers and govtech AI.
insights.quote: the total percentage of tokens in the world used by the public sector is probably 0.01%. Total world GDP public sector is 20%.

insights.claim: Russia has reportedly committed $26 billion to anti-aging research as a national priority, targeting 3D-printed tissues, transplantable organs and epigenetic reprogramming by 2030.
insights.speaker: Peter Diamandis (host)
insights.so_what: State-scale longevity funding signals a new sovereign-backed biotech race; whichever country scales longevity fastest is argued to gain massive GDP growth, a long-horizon thematic.
insights.quote: Russia has committed $26 billion to anti-aging research after making longevity a national priority.

insights.claim: New Limit, the epigenetic-reprogramming startup co-founded by Coinbase CEO Brian Armstrong and Blake Byers, raised $435 million at a $3.1 billion valuation, with human clinical studies targeted for next year starting with alcohol-related liver disease.
insights.speaker: Peter Diamandis (host)
insights.so_what: A concrete, well-capitalized private longevity comp ($3.1B val) with a near-term clinical catalyst; a benchmark for valuing the epigenetic-reprogramming subsector.
insights.quote: New Limit... just raised $435 million and hitting a valuation of $3.1 billion.

insights.claim: Mostaque argues longevity will attract huge capital faster than robotics and is more accessible, urging young people to go 'full on into longevity' as a top wealth-creation vector.
insights.speaker: Imad Mostaque (guest)
insights.so_what: Flags longevity as a faster-growing capital magnet than robotics; a sector-allocation steer for thematic investors and talent.
insights.quote: longevity will have huge amounts of money, even faster than robotics and is more accessible.

insights.claim: Verve 102, a one-time CRISPR base-editing gene therapy delivered via mRNA/LNP that permanently switches off the liver PCSK9 gene, reduced LDL cholesterol by 62% (sustained up to 18 months) and cut PCSK9 protein levels 88% in a NEJM-published Phase 1 trial.
insights.speaker: Peter Diamandis (host)
insights.so_what: A single-shot, durable LDL-lowering gene therapy could disrupt chronic statin/PCSK9-antibody (e.g., Repatha) markets and validate in-vivo base editing; a clinical catalyst with broad cardiometabolic TAM.
insights.quote: In a phase 1 trial published in the New England Journal of Medicine, the highest dose reduced LDL cholesterol by 62%.

insights.claim: Wissner-Gross frames Verve 102's mechanism (derived from rare humans with naturally low-LDL mutations) as a template for many single-injection mRNA/LNP base-editing therapies targeting protective human gene variants for diseases like Alzheimer's and cancer.
insights.speaker: Alex Wissner-Gross (guest)
insights.so_what: Mining protective natural human variants for one-shot base-editing therapies defines a broad pipeline opportunity across major disease categories; a screening framework for gene-therapy investment.
insights.quote: how many other human in varants in in human natural human biodiversity are there for people who never get Alzheimer's never get cancer

themes.theme: How retail can access AI value & media/trust collapse

insights.claim: Diamandis lays out four retail on-ramps to AI value: buy public AI stocks/ETFs (Nvidia, Microsoft, Google, and soon SpaceX, xAI, Anthropic, OpenAI), use AI to multiply your own earning power, build an AI-powered business cheaply, or back government-stake proposals.
insights.speaker: Peter Diamandis (host)
insights.so_what: A practical menu for gaining AI exposure; the explicit naming of imminent public listings (SpaceX, xAI, Anthropic, OpenAI) flags the names to watch for new equity access.
insights.quote: you can buy stock, you can buy Nvidia, Microsoft, Google, soon, space, XAI, anthropic, open AAI, buy it in the public markets

insights.claim: Mostaque proposes AI labs put Anthropic and OpenAI shares into the InvestAmerica funds of every American child as a better alternative to Sanders' 50% stock tax.
insights.speaker: Imad Mostaque (guest)
insights.so_what: An alternative public-ownership mechanism that, if adopted, would channel AI equity to households without forced selling; a policy-design wildcard for AI cap tables.
insights.quote: They should put anthropic and open AI shares into the invest America's funds of every single child in America.

insights.claim: Public trust in media has fallen to ~19% (one in five), down a near-straight line from ~80% in the mid-1970s, with Wissner-Gross projecting it reaches near zero around 2030 as ad revenue loss forces outlets toward sensationalism.
insights.speaker: Peter Diamandis / Alex Wissner-Gross
insights.so_what: Collapsing media trust opens whitespace for AI-built transparent news products and creator-led distribution; a disruption vector for media business models.
insights.quote: Trust in media has hit an all-time low. It's down to 19%.

insights.claim: Blundin reports running ~170 AI agents in parallel for neural-network research and parallelized idea generation, framing learning to orchestrate large agent fleets now as a high-value 'meta' skill for a future of billions of agents.
insights.speaker: Dave Blundin (guest)
insights.so_what: Early experimentation with large agent fleets points to multi-agent orchestration as an emerging capability and tooling category; relevant for agent-infrastructure investing.
insights.quote: the reason I'm running so many agents is because ultimately we're all going to want that many

top_entities: Bernie Sanders AI sovereign wealth fund
